lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market poultry slaughter and processing firms that repeatedly provide inspected, custom, toll, co-processing, cutting, packaging, or value-added processing services to external farms, brands, cooperatives, or food companies.
lens.excluded_activities: Vertically integrated poultry companies processing birds they own, captive plants, businesses principally selling their own poultry products, contract grow-out farms, on-farm self-processing, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat processing for external poultry owners and brands, billed per bird, per pound, per batch, or under negotiated toll/co-processing agreements, sometimes with packaging and specialty-process add-ons.
lens.deviation_from_default: The lens is narrowed because NAICS 311615 is dominated by vertically integrated product companies while a smaller custom and toll-processing subset supplies a recurring outsourced service; combining them would make eligibility and retention incoherent.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 311600 occupation mix rather than wage-weighted task exposure for 311615, while a USDA research program identifies poultry-specific robotic deboning, sensing, contamination detection, and human-in-the-loop work. The estimate maps these targets onto production, quality, maintenance, scheduling, and administrative tasks. It may overstate exposure in small flexible plants and understate it on high-volume standardized lines.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 59.77% of broader-industry employment and cutters/trimmers alone are 18.64%. Poultry's repetitive line work, relatively uniform carcasses, imaging opportunities, and digital traceability create exposure in deboning, inspection support, sorting, packing, sanitation verification, scheduling, and records, while live-bird handling, cleaning, maintenance, and exception work remain difficult.
a.change_evidence: A 311615-specific wage-by-occupation file joined to line-level task studies across small, midsize, and large poultry plants, with current robot cycle time, yield loss, sanitation time, override rates, and staffing data.
caveats: The occupation source includes non-poultry slaughter and processing and is employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted.
Research targets demonstrate exposure, not proven commercial labor substitution.

rho.bridge: USDA's poultry automation program specifies plant-ready goals and explicitly describes the need for large human-data collection, long-term validation, optimization, new sanitization protocols, and food-safety controls; it does not measure five-year industry implementation. The range translates those readiness constraints to LMM processors and may understate adoption of mature administrative and vision tools while overstating advanced robotics deployment.
rho.rationale: Scheduling, records, customer communication, basic vision inspection, and predictive maintenance can deploy within five years. Robotic deboning and other direct product-contact tasks must match human speed and yield, withstand washdown, control pathogen and foreign-object risks, and integrate with variable products and legacy lines, limiting the implemented fraction.
rho.change_evidence: A deployment registry from poultry-equipment vendors and processors tracking commercial installations, plant size, uptime, birds per minute, yield, food-safety events, sanitation burden, labor hours removed, and 24-month retention.
caveats: The cited program is forward-looking research rather than representative adoption evidence.
Implementation will differ materially between software workflows and food-contact robotics.

e.bridge: USDA documents that poultry production is mostly coordinated by integrators that own the birds and handle processing and marketing, while USDA and extension case studies document a smaller external-service processor model. Neither source measures the share of LMM 311615 firms whose qualifying service revenue is material. The estimate may be low if private-label co-processing is underreported and high if niche facilities are below the EBITDA band.
e.rationale: The dominant integrator model falls outside the recurring external-service lens even though growers are contractors. Custom and inspected plants serving independent farmers and brands clearly qualify, but USDA characterizes local processing as a constrained niche and some facilities are their own anchor customers.
e.change_evidence: A current census of the 87 estimated LMM firms using FSIS establishment records, websites, product labels, customer disclosures, and interviews to code third-party throughput and toll/custom revenue as a share of total revenue.
caveats: Broiler organization evidence concerns production systems, not a firm-level processor revenue census.
Small custom plants may not reach the $1-10M EBITDA band; larger co-packers may not advertise customers.

s5.rationale: A qualifying five-year control-transfer probability near one-fifth is a bounded judgment for niche poultry processors. Scarce inspection status, customer relationships, and local capacity can attract buyers, while disease exposure, labor, wastewater, food-safety liability, specialized equipment, integrator power, and owner dependence can instead lead to delayed succession, closure, or an asset sale.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based five-year study combining PitchBook/Capital IQ deals, FSIS ownership and grant-of-inspection changes, state licenses, UCC records, closures, and specialist food-plant broker interviews.
caveats: No industry-specific transfer-rate series was found.
Facility sales, label-owner changes, and family succession may not be qualifying control transfers.

q.bridge: USDA and extension case studies show fee-for-service processing, anchor customers, scarce local capacity, variable pricing, and per-bird charges, but do not measure five-year retention of AI benefits. The range infers sharing through fee negotiations and competition; niche local examples may overstate scarcity and pricing power relative to larger co-processing contracts.
q.rationale: Per-bird, per-pound, and negotiated processing fees do not mechanically pass labor savings through, and limited inspected capacity plus customer-specific certifications can slow switching. At renewal, sophisticated brands can demand lower fees, competitors can adopt similar tools, and transparent unit pricing exposes productivity gains, so retention is meaningful but not complete.
q.change_evidence: Processor contracts, bids, and customer-level invoices before and after automation, including escalation clauses, minimum-volume commitments, change-order pricing, capacity utilization, churn, and contribution margins.
caveats: Published small-plant charges do not represent confidential LMM co-processing agreements.
Commodity product sales and processor-owned throughput can obscure true service margins.

d5.bridge: USDA projects national poultry production, not constant-price demand for the eligible outsourced processor basket over exactly 2026-2031. The range translates projected broiler and turkey throughput into service demand; it may overstate eligible demand if growth stays inside integrated plants and understate it if independent brands outsource more processing.
d5.rationale: USDA projects chicken production increasing from 45.7 billion pounds in 2022 to 52.5 billion in 2033 and turkey production recovering 14% from its depressed 2022 level by 2033. Broiler demand and population support growth, tempered by turkey's declining per-capita consumption, avian-influenza risk, exports, feed costs, and vertical integration.
d5.change_evidence: The latest USDA 2026-2031 baseline volumes by broiler and turkey, FSIS establishment-level slaughter and processing volumes, independent-brand sales forecasts, and eligible-processor backlog and utilization data.
caveats: The USDA baseline assumes no further animal-disease outbreaks and continuation of existing policies and trade agreements.
Industry production growth need not accrue to independent service processors.

o.bridge: Federal rules and USDA research establish physical inspection, sanitation, product-control, and robotics requirements but do not measure the future operator-required quantity. The estimate treats slaughter, custody, chilling, cutting, sanitation, packaging, and accountable compliance as non-software services. It may overstate the eligible share if producers self-process under exemptions or brands integrate processing.
o.rationale: Poultry processing requires physical transformation under sanitary and inspection controls, with plant personnel authorized to halt production and correct deficiencies. AI and robotics can reduce staffing and improve decisions, but customers still require an accountable inspected or legally exempt operator rather than software alone within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence from regulator-approved highly autonomous plants showing unattended operating hours, intervention rates, sanitation and pathogen outcomes, plus producer adoption of on-farm exempt processing and customer vertical integration.
caveats: Operator-required demand can shift from outsourced processors to self-processing or integrated plants.
Different federal and state exemptions create heterogeneous compliance burdens and market access.

narrative.executive_view: Poultry processing has credible AI exposure because work is repetitive, high-throughput, and increasingly sensor-friendly. The investable lens is much narrower than the code: only custom, toll, and co-processors serving external owners qualify, while the dominant vertically integrated processors do not.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term gains are strongest in scheduling, records, quality imaging, contamination detection, yield optimization, predictive maintenance, and guided inspection. Direct robotic deboning is promising but still must meet human line speed and yield while surviving washdown and controlling new food-safety risks.
narrative.value_capture: Unit processing fees and scarce inspected capacity support initial retention, particularly where customers need certifications or consistent slots. Renewal bargaining, transparent per-bird economics, customer scale, and replication by competing processors will share benefits over time.
narrative.firm_availability: Most industry capacity is tied to integrators that own birds and control processing and marketing. The qualifying subset consists of independent plants serving farms, brands, and aggregators; some are too small for the EBITDA band and others rely heavily on self-owned throughput, making eligibility and transfer supply uncertain.
narrative.demand_durability: Broiler production is projected to grow steadily, and turkey output is expected to recover, supporting physical throughput. Disease outbreaks, feed and export shocks, weakening turkey consumption, and growth captured by integrated plants remain material risks for eligible processors.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main diligence gaps are third-party revenue share, commercial automation performance at LMM scale, and control-transfer frequency. Food-safety events, avian influenza, wastewater, labor, line downtime, yield loss, customer concentration, and integrator competition can overwhelm savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Repetitive labor-intensive processing with strong vision, sensing, workflow, and selective robotics applicability against growing broiler volume.
narrative.principal_weakness: The qualifying independent service subset is likely small because vertically integrated processors dominate poultry throughput.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311600
sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation mix: production 59.77%, cutters and trimmers 18.64%, slaughterers and meat packers 12.20%, and maintenance and repair 6.47%.

sources.title: Center for Scalable and Intelligent Automation in Poultry Processing
sources.supports: USDA program identifies AI, robotics, sensing, robotic deboning, human-in-the-loop work, contamination detection, and the speed, yield, validation, sanitation, and food-safety challenges of automation.

sources.title: Financial Risks and Incomes in Contract Broiler Production
sources.supports: USDA describes the integrator model: almost all broilers are raised under contract, while poultry companies supply birds and feed and handle processing and marketing.

sources.title: Solving Processing Issues a Key to Successful Local Meat Marketing
sources.supports: USDA documents external poultry-processing services, anchor customers, bottleneck capacity, farmer commitments, variable pricing, scheduling tools, and small-processor throughput challenges.

sources.title: Plant in a Box: A Solution for USDA-Inspected Poultry Processing?
sources.supports: Extension case study reports external processing customers, per-bird and per-pound fees, labor intensity, throughput, seasonal staffing, and the economics of a small inspected poultry plant.

sources.title: Long-Term Growth Projected as U.S. Poultry and Egg Sector Recovers
sources.supports: USDA projects chicken production from 45.7 billion pounds in 2022 to 52.5 billion in 2033 and turkey production up 14% from 2022 by 2033, with explicit disease and policy assumptions.

sources.title: 9 CFR 381.145: Poultry Products Entering or at Official Establishments
sources.supports: Federal rules require inspected and identified product, examination, operator identification at receipt, quality-control records, corrective action, and accountable establishment personnel.
